evidence: Layers are well-defined: model → repository → service → controller, with config as cross-cutting. No obvious upward dependency violations.
AuthCodeStore and MicrosoftChallengeStore interfaces live in the service package rather than a dedicated ports/interfaces package — mild boundary blur.
Controller DTOs (MicrosoftChallengeResponse, MicrosoftVerifyRequest) are properly isolated in controller/dto/.
AuthController depends on 7 service-layer beans which is high but justified for an orchestration controller.
No import cycles detected.

service package contains both interfaces (AuthCodeStore, MicrosoftChallengeStore) and implementations (Redis*) and domain objects (NormalizedIdentity, AuthenticatedUserIdentity) — mixed concerns in one package.
The scan flags 25 orphaned files but this is a Java Spring app where Spring wires beans at runtime; the static importer analysis underestimates actual coupling.
Top-level packages (config, controller, model, repository, service) map cleanly to Spring conventions and are predictable for any Spring developer.

AuthController is the single largest class at 222 LOC and handles Google client-side, Microsoft client-side, auth code exchange, logout, and Microsoft challenge issuance — five distinct responsibilities.
No README or architecture doc exists beyond skeleton README.md (1 byte).
JwtTokenService handles both token minting (v1 and v2 paths) and parsing/validation — dual responsibility that blurs its ownership.
The frontend has clear page/component/hook/api separation — good decomposition.

The 'config' package mixes Spring configuration beans (SecurityConfig), filter implementations (JwtAuthenticationFilter, MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter), property holders (MicrosoftAuthProperties, AuthRolloutProperties), and a startup guard (IdentityContractGuard).
Java backend uses consistent camelCase for methods/fields and PascalCase for classes — no naming convention drift.
Frontend uses consistent camelCase throughout TypeScript files.
All Java classes use constructor injection (not field injection) consistently — good convention adherence.
AuthCodeStore and MicrosoftChallengeStore interfaces have consistent documentation style with clear Javadoc contracts.
MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter is missing a newline between class closing brace and next class in the concatenated output, but this is a file boundary artifact not an actual issue.
controller/dto/ has only 2 files both using record types — consistent.
ProviderIdentityNormalizer uses a private static isBlank helper; AuthController also defines a private static isBlank — duplicated helper across two files.

User entity uses standard Java getters/setters while newer code uses records (NormalizedIdentity, AuthenticatedUserIdentity, MicrosoftChallengeResponse, MicrosoftVerifyRequest) — two type strategies for domain objects, but this is intentional: JPA entity vs immutable value objects.
Service layer throws IllegalArgumentException for validation failures (GoogleTokenVerifier, MicrosoftTokenVerifier, ProviderIdentityNormalizer) — consistent domain-level error type.
Controller layer catches exceptions and returns structured JSON error responses — appropriate boundary handling.
OAuth2SuccessHandler.normalizeIdentity() catches IllegalArgumentException and redirects to frontend error URL — loses the exception but this is intentional UI-layer handling.
AuthCodeStore.exchangeCode() returns null for invalid/expired codes. AuthController then checks for null. This null-return pattern is inconsistent with the throw pattern used in tokenVerifiers.
UserController.getCurrentUser() returns 404 via Optional.map/orElse — clean.
error strategy_by_directory shows service/ has both throws (23), try_catch (5), and returns_null (5) — mixed strategy within the same layer.
UserService.findOrCreateUser catches DataIntegrityViolationException and rethrows as IllegalStateException — good context preservation.
MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter writes raw JSON string literal for error responses rather than using ResponseEntity or a shared error body formatter.
Domain names are precise: NormalizedIdentity, AuthenticatedUserIdentity, ProviderIdentityNormalizer, AuthCodeStore — all communicate intent clearly.
AuthFlow enum (SERVER_SIDE, CLIENT_SIDE) and AuthProvider enum (GOOGLE, MICROSOFT) are clear and well-named.
JwtMintMode and IdentityContractMode enum names are domain-specific and descriptive.
isBlank is a reasonable name for the private helper despite being duplicated.
normalizeGoogleClaims and normalizeMicrosoftClaims in ProviderIdentityNormalizer are clear about what they normalize.
MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter name is accurate but verbose — 'authorization gate' is specific.
IdentityContractGuard clearly communicates startup enforcement semantics.
Frontend: useAuth, useProviderConfig, apiFetch are all clear.

mergeProviderConfig in types/auth.ts is slightly misleading — it doesn't merge two configs, it fills in defaults for a partial config. A better name would be normalizeProviderConfig or applyProviderConfigDefaults.
legacyGoogleCompatibilityId in UserService is well-named and communicates the legacy/compat purpose.
Variable name 'registrationId' in OAuth2SuccessHandler is Spring-idiomatic and accurate.

AuthCodeStore and MicrosoftChallengeStore interfaces each have exactly one concrete implementation (Redis). Single-implementation interfaces are justified here by the @Profile('!test') pattern enabling in-memory test substitutes.
ProviderIdentityNormalizer normalizes claims from multiple providers into a single NormalizedIdentity — earns its abstraction by insulating controllers from provider-specific attribute names.
NormalizedIdentity and AuthenticatedUserIdentity are lean records with no over-engineering.
JwtTokenService encapsulates V1/V2 token version differences — appropriate abstraction for controlled rollout.
AuthRolloutProperties aggregates rollout flags — earns its existence by enabling multi-flag coordinated feature toggles.
pom.xml uses spring-boot-starter-parent for version management — appropriate.
google-api-client 2.7.2 used only for GoogleIdTokenVerifier — correct purpose-built library.
NimbusJwtDecoder from spring-security-oauth2-resource-server used for Microsoft JWT decoding — correct.
jjwt 0.12.6 provides internal JWT minting/parsing.
No duplicate-purpose libraries detected.
Frontend: @react-oauth/google and @azure/msal-browser are correct official SDKs for each provider.
JwtTokenService.parseAuthenticatedUser() has a clear early-return structure: check version, dispatch to legacy or v2 path — intention-revealing control flow.
ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeGoogleClaims() and .normalizeMicrosoftClaims() are focused and well-structured.
AuthController.verifyMicrosoftToken() has 5 sequential guard clauses before the happy path — slightly verbose but each guards a distinct precondition.
MicrosoftTokenVerifier.verifyIdToken() decomposes validation into 4 focused private methods (validateAudience, validateIssuer, validateVersion, validateNonce) — very clean.
ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeEmail() has a complex validation condition with 5 subexpressions in one if — could be decomposed.
UserService.findOrCreateUser() handles the lookup-then-create pattern with a race-condition fallback on DataIntegrityViolationException — sophisticated but justified and clearly commented.
AuthController.cookiePath() has a redundant null check: servletContextPath is already normalized to empty string in constructor, so the null check inside cookiePath() is dead code.
UserController.getCurrentUser() uses a large Map.of() with 10 entries inline — should use a response DTO record instead.
DashboardPage.tsx getAvatarInitials function is clean and well-structured with precise edge case handling.
useAuth hook in frontend is concise and uses correct state initialization patterns.
OAuth2SuccessHandler.onAuthenticationSuccess → normalizeIdentity → ProviderIdentityNormalizer is a clean handoff chain with clear data translation at each seam.
AuthController: Google verify flow is: parse body → verify token → normalize identity → find/create user → generate JWT → return token. Clean linear handoff.
AuthController: Microsoft verify flow has 5 pre-flight checks spread as sequential if-returns before delegating to services — the guard progression is readable but somewhat scattered.
The auth code exchange seam (OAuth2SuccessHandler stores JWT → authCodeStore → AuthController.exchangeCode) is a clean single-use token redemption pattern.
JwtTokenService generates token for User, but OAuth2SuccessHandler calls jwtTokenService.generateToken(user) after finding or creating the user — the handoff is straightforward.
UserController.getCurrentUser: identity is obtained from Security context → findCurrentUser(identity) → map to response. Clean pipeline.

The Microsoft challenge/verify two-step protocol has a slightly complex session cookie handoff between issueMicrosoftChallenge and verifyMicrosoftToken — workable but the session ID extraction is spread across both endpoints.
Frontend: HomePage fetches providers config → conditionally renders login card components → components call back with token → useAuth.login stores it. Clean data flow.

Tests found: JwtAuthenticationFilterTest, MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilterTest, JwtTokenServiceTest, GoogleTokenVerifierTest, UserServiceTest, OAuth2SuccessHandlerTest, AuthFlowTest, ProviderConfigControllerTest, MicrosoftServerSideIntegrationTest, UserControllerTest.
JwtTokenServiceTest covers V1 and V2 token generation, parsing, and validation — good coverage of the core JWT path.
UserServiceTest covers findOrCreateUser for Google and Microsoft providers — good.
GoogleTokenVerifierTest exists but has unused imports (GoogleIdToken, Assertions) suggesting incomplete test development.
OAuth2SuccessHandlerTest has unused import ArgumentMatchers — similarly suggests incomplete test development.
ProviderConfigControllerTest has 4 unit tests covering the enabled/disabled config logic — good.
MicrosoftServerSideIntegrationTest is a @SpringBootTest integration test that validates the OAuth2 redirect — good integration coverage for that path.
JwtAuthenticationFilterTest and MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilterTest have unused Assertions and Mockito imports — test development may be incomplete.

No tests for: ProviderIdentityNormalizer (critical — email normalization, guest identity rejection), MicrosoftTokenVerifier (critical — nonce validation, issuer validation), RedisAuthCodeStore (important — code exchange logic), RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore, AuthCodeStore exchange flow.
UserController.getCurrentUser has no test for the identity fallback path (non-AuthenticatedUserIdentity principal).
The scan correctly identified 25 untested modules — but many are Spring-wired services that are effectively tested through integration tests.
All controller endpoints return ResponseEntity<?> with wildcard type — loses type information at the API boundary.
AuthController: /google/verify, /microsoft/verify return ResponseEntity<?> with either Map<String,String> body or error body — inconsistent response shape depending on success/failure.
POST /auth/exchange returns Map.of("token", jwt) and POST /auth/google/verify returns Map.of("token", jwt) — consistent token response shape across auth flows.
GET /user/me returns Map<String, Object> with 10 fields — no typed response class.
/auth/providers returns Map<String, Object> — untyped response.
AuthController constructor takes 9 parameters including @Value injected servletContextPath — large constructor parameter list.
ProviderConfigController.getProviders() builds response with two nested LinkedHashMaps with boolean+string fields — not type-safe.
Frontend API client apiFetch<T> is generic and typed — good.
Frontend types/auth.ts defines GoogleProviderConfig and MicrosoftProviderConfig interfaces — typed contracts for the provider API.
SecurityConfig.filterChain() explicitly permits /oauth2/**, /login/oauth2/**, /auth/** and requires authentication for /user/** and anyRequest — consistent at the route level.
JwtAuthenticationFilter extracts and validates JWT for all requests — cross-cutting.',
MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter checks rollout feature flags before allowing Microsoft OAuth flows — consistent gate.
IdentityContractGuard enforces at startup that Microsoft SSO requires V2_ONLY identity contract — prevents misconfiguration.

AuthController validates rolloutProperties.getMicrosoft().isClientSideEnabled() in both /microsoft/challenge and /microsoft/verify independently — the feature flag check is duplicated across endpoints rather than centralized.
AuthController checks rollout flag and returns 503 in two different places for the same Microsoft client-side disabled condition — consistent error code but duplicated gate logic.
UserController has no explicit authorization annotation (@PreAuthorize) — relies entirely on SecurityConfig path matching for authenticated access.
No permission string literals or magic strings used — feature flags accessed through typed property classes.
AuthCodeStore.java comment_ratio: 0.58 — highest in codebase. Javadoc explains single-use semantics and null-return contract, not restating code.
RedisAuthCodeStore: comment '// GETDEL: atomically get and delete — guarantees single-use' — explains non-obvious Redis operation.
AuthController.guard_density: 8.0 — 8 guard clauses. Each guards a real precondition, not defensive overengineering.
No entry/exit logging on every function.
No defensive null checks on clearly non-nullable values in happy paths.
No generic handle/process/do names.
No boilerplate error handling copied verbatim across files.
isBlank() helper appears in two files — duplication, not AI-generated bloat.
The V1/V2 JWT contract migration is explicitly in-progress and controlled by AuthRolloutProperties.JwtMintMode and IdentityContractMode — this is an active tracked migration, not stale debt.
AuthenticatedUserIdentity has legacy() and v2() factory methods — legitimate compatibility shims for the active migration.
JwtTokenService.generateToken(User) dispatches on jwtMintMode — the v1 path will be removed when migration is complete.
User entity still has googleId field (String) alongside the generic provider/providerUserId pattern — legacy Google-only identity storage alongside the new multi-provider model.

UserService.findOrCreateUser() has Google-specific legacy fallback: 'if (existing.isEmpty() && identity.provider() == AuthProvider.GOOGLE) { existing = userRepository.findByGoogleId(identity.providerUserId()); }' — explicit migration compat code.
User.loginMethod String field coexists with User.lastLoginFlow AuthFlow enum — two representations of the same login concept.
No TODO/FIXME referencing 'legacy' or 'migrate' found — migration is tracked through the rollout properties, not left as TODO comments.
No mixed JS/TS files in frontend — fully TypeScript.
Top-level packages: config (6 files, 348 LOC), controller (3 files + dto/ subdir), model (3 files), repository (1 file), service (12 files, 823 LOC).
service/ has 12 files — the largest package by file count, containing interfaces, implementations, value objects, and domain services all co-located.
config/ contains 6 files including 2 filter implementations — filters are not configuration.
controller/dto/ is a well-placed subpackage for request/response types.
The backend root package contains only SsoApplication.java — correct.
service/ should be split: service/store/ (AuthCodeStore, MicrosoftChallengeStore + Redis impls), service/identity/ (NormalizedIdentity, AuthenticatedUserIdentity, ProviderIdentityNormalizer).
Frontend structure: api/, components/, hooks/, pages/, types/ — appropriate flat structure for a small project.
Frontend is small enough (<15 files) that the flat structure is appropriate — no split needed.
No import-affinity mismatch found — files are generally in appropriate packages.
GoogleTokenVerifier creates GoogleIdTokenVerifier in its constructor — lightweight builder with no network calls at construction.
MicrosoftTokenVerifier.buildDecoder() builds NimbusJwtDecoder at construction — JWK key fetch is lazy on first use.
IdentityContractGuard uses @PostConstruct for startup validation — clean pattern.
No environment variable reads at class load time.
No circular init dependencies — Spring constructor injection resolves all ordering.
AuthRolloutProperties uses @ConfigurationProperties — bound after context startup, not at class load time.
RedisAuthCodeStore reads rolloutProperties at call time, not at construction.
AuthController has 8 constructor parameters and handles 5 distinct endpoint operations — doing too many things for a single class.
MicrosoftTokenVerifier has a clean internal design: constructor delegates to buildDecoder factory, validation split into 4 private methods — coherent.

ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeGoogleClaims has 5 params (googleId, email, name, pictureUrl, loginFlow) + ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeMicrosoftClaims has claims map + loginFlow — the claims Map<String,Object> param is appropriate for dynamic OAuth attribute maps.
UserService.findOrCreateUser() is 40+ LOC doing: lookup by provider, Google legacy fallback lookup, update existing, create new with race-condition fallback — 3 distinct operations in one method.
JwtTokenService.generateToken() dispatches on JwtMintMode — the dual-version token generation is coherent as a single 'emit a token for this user' responsibility.
IdentityContractGuard.validate() has 4 consecutive validation blocks — focused single responsibility.

AuthController.verifyMicrosoftToken() has 5 sequential guard clauses then delegates — the guards are appropriate but the method could be broken into validateMicrosoftRequest() + performMicrosoftVerification().
UserService: findOrCreateUser(), findByEmail(), findCurrentUser() — three public methods, coherent user management responsibility.
DashboardPage renders avatar, profile card, and logout — appropriate for a page component.
AuthCodeStore interface has clear Javadoc contracts: storeJwt returns a code, exchangeCode returns null if invalid/expired.
The null-return contract of AuthCodeStore.exchangeCode() diverges from the throw convention used by sibling services — already noted in error_consistency.
MicrosoftChallengeStore interface has no Javadoc — the contracts of issueChallenge() and consumeNonce() are undocumented at the interface level.
JwtTokenService.parseAuthenticatedUser() returns AuthenticatedUserIdentity but can also throw IllegalArgumentException and JwtException — the exception contract is undocumented.
JwtTokenService.isTokenValid() returns boolean and never throws — contract is clear from the name.
AuthenticatedUserIdentity.legacy() factory creates an object where userId is null — callers must check isLegacy() before accessing userId. This implicit contract could be documented.

UserService.findCurrentUser() takes an AuthenticatedUserIdentity but uses identity.email() for legacy and identity.userId() for v2 — the branching is hidden inside the method, not evident from the signature.
ProviderIdentityNormalizer methods throw IllegalArgumentException for invalid inputs — consistent and appropriate.
MicrosoftChallenge record is declared inside MicrosoftChallengeStore interface — clean co-location of related contract type.

JwtTokenService.parseAuthenticatedUser() control flow: check version null → legacy path; check version != 2 → throw; check acceptsV2 → throw; extract and validate claims → return. Clear decision tree.
JwtTokenService.isTokenValid() catches JwtException and IllegalArgumentException and returns false — clean boolean wrapper.

AuthController.verifyMicrosoftToken() has clearly distinct guard clauses: feature flag, input validation, session cookie, consumed nonce, verifier availability — each guards a real precondition, no redundancy.

UserService.findOrCreateUser() Google legacy fallback: 'if (existing.isEmpty() && identity.provider() == AuthProvider.GOOGLE) { existing = userRepository.findByGoogleId(identity.providerUserId()); }' — the GOOGLE-specific branch is explicit and correct.

ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeEmail(): complex condition 'normalized.isBlank() || count('@') != 1 || contains(' ') || atIndex <= 0 || atIndex == normalized.length() - 1' — correct but complex 5-clause OR is hard to parse at a glance.
RedisAuthCodeStore.exchangeCode(): multi-path lookup (LEGACY_ONLY → v2_key, otherwise try legacy then v2 if acceptsV2) — slightly complex but documented with comment about GETDEL semantics.
MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter: two separate path checks with .endsWith() — no dead code paths.
AuthController.cookiePath(): redundant null check (servletContextPath already non-null from constructor) creates a technically dead branch.
Java is a statically typed language — most type safety concerns apply to unchecked casts and raw types.

AuthController: Map<String, String> body parameter for /google/verify — the credential is accessed via body.get("credential") which returns null for missing keys, handled with null check. Appropriate.
The unchecked cast @SuppressWarnings("unchecked") Map<String, Object> google = (Map<String, Object>) response.getBody().get("google") in test code — acceptable in tests.
ProviderConfigController returns Map<String, Object> — untyped response, could be typed DTO.

ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeMicrosoftClaims(): Map<String, Object> claims parameter uses string key access with String.valueOf(claims.get(key)) — dynamic but safe since it handles null via stringClaim helper.
AuthenticatedUserIdentity record: userId field is Long (nullable) — callers must check isLegacy() before accessing. Not enforced by type system.
Frontend: mergeProviderConfig accepts Partial<ProviderConfig> | null — proper TypeScript narrowing.
Frontend: apiFetch<T> is generic and typed — good.
Frontend types/auth.ts defines interfaces for all provider config shapes — good type coverage.
useAuth hook UserProfile interface doesn't include provider/providerUserId fields that the backend now returns — TypeScript interface is incomplete/stale.
ResponseEntity<?> wildcard in controllers loses generics — see api_surface_coherence.

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: The architecture follows a standard Spring layered pattern cleanly without cycles, but the service package has grown into a mixed-concern dumping ground holding interfaces, implementations, value objects, and domain records together. There are no hub modules with blast-radius concern, and dependency direction is consistently controller → service → repository → model.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: Score 72: The layering is healthy and direction violations are absent, which is the most important property. The primary defect is the service package mixing interfaces, Redis implementations, and identity records without subpackaging — a navigability and boundary clarity issue that falls short of a well-organized codebase but does not create dependency cycles.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The structural decomposition into config/controller/model/repository/service follows Spring idiom and is immediately comprehensible. However, the AuthController has grown into a multi-provider orchestration hub doing too much, and the config package mixes disparate concerns (filters, property classes, startup validation) behind a single label that overpromises cohesion.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: Score 68: The package layout is conventional and discoverable, but two sources of ownership blur (AuthController doing 5 things, config housing filters + properties + validation) prevent a higher score. A new engineer would understand the shape quickly but find the config and AuthController boundaries fuzzy.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The codebase follows consistent naming and injection conventions throughout. The most notable outlier is the duplicated isBlank helper method appearing in both AuthController and ProviderIdentityNormalizer — a small but real convention gap. The JPA entity vs record duality is intentional and well-reasoned.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: Score 80: Strong convention adherence with no naming drift or organizational islands. The duplicated utility method is the clearest defect. Overall, a consistent first-time-reader experience.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The codebase has a generally sound error strategy — service layer throws typed exceptions, controllers catch and map to HTTP responses. The main inconsistency is that AuthCodeStore returns null for not-found codes while token verifiers throw IllegalArgumentException for the analogous case, creating mixed patterns for callers. Additionally, a few filter-level error responses use raw string JSON instead of a shared formatter.
error_consistency.score_rationale: Score 65: The overall strategy is coherent but the null-return from AuthCodeStore vs throw from verifiers forces callers to use two different error-handling patterns. Raw JSON strings in filters are a lesser concern. These are fixable with minimal change.

naming_quality.dimension_character: Naming across this codebase is notably strong — domain concepts (identity, provider, flow, contract) are reflected in class and method names consistently with no generic verbs or opaque abbreviations. The single notable outlier is mergeProviderConfig in the frontend types which overpromises what the function does.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Score 82: High-quality naming discipline throughout both backend and frontend. Only one name misrepresents behavior (mergeProviderConfig doing defaults-application, not merging two sources). No generic handle/process/do names found.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: Most abstractions in this codebase pay for themselves. ProviderIdentityNormalizer is the clearest win. The single-implementation interfaces are justified by the test profile pattern. No empty wrapper chains or wide option bags detected.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Score 75: Most abstractions are appropriately sized. The single-implementation interface pattern is legitimate for the test profile strategy. The service package grouping makes abstraction/implementation boundaries harder to navigate visually but does not constitute an abstraction defect.

dependency_health.dimension_character: Both backend and frontend dependency choices are well-matched to use cases with no duplicate-purpose libraries. Backend uses the Spring ecosystem coherently with appropriate third-party additions. Frontend uses official provider SDKs.
dependency_health.score_rationale: Score 78: Clean dependency choices with no conflicts or duplication. Minor note: google-api-client is heavy for only ID token verification; Spring OAuth2 resource server could handle this at the framework level. This is an optimization, not a defect.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: Most function internals are direct and intention-revealing, with good use of early returns and focused private method extraction (especially in MicrosoftTokenVerifier). The main internal craft issues are a complex multi-condition email validator expression, a redundant null check in cookiePath(), and UserController returning an inline Map with 10 entries instead of a typed DTO.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Score 72: Generally clean internals with a few specific craft gaps. None are severe but they add friction and reduce readability at specific call sites.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: The main orchestration flows (OAuth2 success → identity normalization → user upsert → JWT → redirect/response) are clean composable pipelines. The Microsoft challenge two-step creates some session/cookie state that is managed inline in AuthController rather than in a dedicated component, resulting in a moderately tangled seam between endpoints.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: Score 70: Most handoffs are explicit and minimal. The main friction point is the Microsoft challenge session state management being spread across two controller endpoints and implemented inline rather than through a dedicated challenge coordinator. This is a mid-level seam design issue, not a breaking problem.

test_strategy.dimension_character: The test suite has a reasonable foundation covering key services (JWT, UserService, filter behavior) but has critical gaps in ProviderIdentityNormalizer and MicrosoftTokenVerifier — the two classes with the most complex business logic (email normalization, nonce validation, issuer validation). Several test files also have unused imports suggesting incomplete test development.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Score 52: Core JWT and user creation paths are tested. Critical gaps in email normalization (ProviderIdentityNormalizer) and Microsoft token validation (MicrosoftTokenVerifier) where incorrect logic would cause security or UX failures represent high-value missing coverage. Unused imports in test files suggest incomplete implementation.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The API shapes are functionally consistent (all auth flows return a token in the same structure) but the implementation uses raw Maps throughout instead of typed response DTOs, losing compile-time safety and making the API contract implicit. The frontend compensates with TypeScript interfaces but the backend offers no typed contract.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: Score 70: The semantic consistency (all flows return {token}) is good, but pervasive use of Map<String,Object> and ResponseEntity<?> wildcards means the API contract is maintained only by convention, not by the type system. Adding response DTOs would be a meaningful improvement.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: Authorization is consistently applied — all protected routes require authentication via SecurityConfig, feature flags are checked through typed properties, and startup validation prevents misconfigured states. The main issue is that the Microsoft client-side feature flag is checked redundantly in two separate endpoints rather than centrally.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: Score 78: The authorization model is well-structured and consistently applied. The duplicated feature flag check in AuthController is a minor maintenance concern. No missing auth gates or permission string magic literals found.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase is largely free of AI-generated debt patterns. Comments explain WHY rather than restating code. Guard density is high in AuthController but justified by real preconditions. The AuthCodeStore Javadocs have high comment ratio but they document intent, not noise.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: Score 76: Clean code with purposeful comments and named helpers. Minor deduction for AuthCodeStore method-level Javadocs that partially restate method names rather than adding unique insight.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The V1→V2 JWT contract migration is the dominant migration story and it is actively managed with explicit feature flags and startup validation. The Google-Id legacy compatibility shims in UserService and User entity are intentional and necessary until data migration completes. The main concern is User.loginMethod (String) and User.lastLoginFlow (AuthFlow enum) duplicating the same concept in the entity.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: Score 72: The in-progress migration is well-managed with proper rollout flags. The User entity's dual login-concept representation (loginMethod String + lastLoginFlow AuthFlow enum) is the clearest technical debt from an incomplete migration, representing legacy data that could be cleaned up by removing loginMethod once all rows have lastLoginFlow populated.

package_organization.dimension_character: The top-level package layout follows standard Spring idioms and is immediately navigable. The service package is too wide and would benefit from subpackaging to separate storage abstractions from identity domain objects. The config package also houses filters which are processing components, not configuration.
package_organization.score_rationale: Score 70: The package structure is discoverable but the service package mixing 12 files of different concerns is the main organizational issue. This impedes navigation as the codebase grows.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The codebase uses constructor injection throughout with no module-level side effects at import time. @PostConstruct startup validation in IdentityContractGuard is a clean pattern. No global singleton ordering concerns.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Score 82: Clean initialization with no hidden ordering dependencies or import-time I/O. The only minor note is GoogleTokenVerifier creates a somewhat heavy object in the constructor, though without network calls.

design_coherence.dimension_character: Most individual classes have coherent designs. The main coherence issues are AuthController (too wide — multiple auth flows in one class) and UserService.findOrCreateUser() (too much in one method — lookup logic, creation logic, and race-condition handling mixed together). These are related to high-level decomposition choices.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Score 68: Several classes have focused, coherent designs (MicrosoftTokenVerifier, IdentityContractGuard, JwtTokenService). The two outliers are the AuthController breadth and findOrCreateUser() length, both of which exceed single-responsibility at their respective levels.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: Most primary contracts are declared clearly. The main contract coherence gaps are: AuthCodeStore's null-return contract vs throw convention (already flagged), missing Javadoc on MicrosoftChallengeStore interface methods, and the implicit contract that AuthenticatedUserIdentity.legacy() objects have nullable userId requiring isLegacy() checks before access.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Score 74: Core contracts are well-defined by naming and documented interfaces. The defects are at the edges — interface method documentation gaps and the implicit nullable-field contract of the legacy identity pattern.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: Control flow throughout the codebase is generally clear and intention-revealing. Guard clauses are used appropriately, decision trees are well-structured, and no identical branches or always-true conditions were found. The main clarity gaps are the complex multi-clause email validation condition and the redundant null check in cookiePath().
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Score 78: Solid logic clarity throughout. Two specific spots (complex normalizeEmail condition, dead null check in cookiePath) reduce the score but are isolated. No systemic logic issues.

type_safety.dimension_character: The backend is typed at the class level (records, enums, typed service methods) but loses type safety at controller boundaries through Map<String,Object> and ResponseEntity<?>. The frontend TypeScript types are mostly well-defined but the useAuth UserProfile interface is missing fields the backend now returns. AuthenticatedUserIdentity's nullable userId requires implicit knowledge of isLegacy().
type_safety.score_rationale: Score 72: Java's static typing protects most domain logic. The main type safety gaps are at the controller API boundary (untyped Maps) and the frontend's stale UserProfile interface. These are addressable without architectural change.

issues.summary: service package mixes interfaces, Redis implementations, and identity value objects without subpackaging
issues.suggestion: Split service/ into subpackages: service/store/ for AuthCodeStore+MicrosoftChallengeStore interfaces and their Redis impls, service/identity/ for NormalizedIdentity+AuthenticatedUserIdentity+ProviderIdentityNormalizer, and keep the remaining services in service/.
evidence: service/ contains 12 files spanning: 2 interfaces (AuthCodeStore, MicrosoftChallengeStore), 2 Redis implementations, 2 identity value records (NormalizedIdentity, AuthenticatedUserIdentity), 2 token verifiers, and 2 identity-handling services.
NormalizedIdentity and AuthenticatedUserIdentity are domain value objects that don't belong co-located with service implementations.

issues.summary: AuthController conflates Google, Microsoft client-side, challenge issuance, code exchange, and logout into one class
issues.suggestion: Split into MicrosoftAuthController (challenge + verify) and a leaner AuthController (exchange, logout, Google verify). The cookie session logic can become a ChallengeSessionManager helper.
evidence: AuthController has 5 endpoints: /google/verify, /microsoft/challenge, /microsoft/verify, /exchange, /logout — each representing a distinct flow.
Class injects 8 dependencies, which is a structural signal that ownership is too wide.
Microsoft challenge session cookie logic (currentChallengeSessionId, createChallengeSessionCookie, cookiePath) adds ~40 LOC of session management that could be a separate component.

issues.summary: config package groups filters, property holders, and startup guard under one ambiguous label
issues.suggestion: Move JwtAuthenticationFilter and MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter to a security/ or filter/ subpackage. IdentityContractGuard could stay in config or move to a startup/ package.
evidence: config/ holds: 2 request filters (JwtAuthenticationFilter, MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter), 2 property classes, 1 startup validator (IdentityContractGuard), and SecurityConfig.
Filters are runtime processing components, not configuration; they belong in a filter/ or security/ subpackage.

issues.summary: private static isBlank() helper duplicated in AuthController and ProviderIdentityNormalizer
issues.suggestion: Replace both with String.isBlank() preceded by a null check using Objects.isNull, or use the standard library directly (value == null || value.isBlank()) inline. Alternatively, create a shared StringUtils class in a util/ package if more utilities accumulate.
evidence: AuthController.isBlank: return value == null || value.isBlank()
ProviderIdentityNormalizer.isBlank: return value == null || value.isBlank()
Both are private static and identical in behavior.

issues.summary: AuthCodeStore.exchangeCode() returns null for not-found codes while sibling services throw IllegalArgumentException
issues.suggestion: Change AuthCodeStore.exchangeCode() to throw IllegalArgumentException (or a custom CodeExpiredException extends IllegalArgumentException) on invalid/expired codes, consistent with the throw convention in sibling services. Update AuthController to use try-catch instead of null check.
evidence: AuthCodeStore Javadoc: 'Returns null if invalid/expired.'
GoogleTokenVerifier.verify(): throws IllegalArgumentException for invalid token.
MicrosoftTokenVerifier.verifyIdToken(): throws IllegalArgumentException for all validation failures.
AuthController.exchangeCode() must null-check the result — if (jwt == null) — while Google/Microsoft flows use try-catch.

issues.summary: MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter writes raw JSON string literals for error responses instead of shared formatter
issues.suggestion: Extract a static writeJsonError(HttpServletResponse response, int status, String message) helper in a FilterResponseHelper class and use it consistently in all filters and the authentication entry point.
evidence: response.getWriter().write("{\"error\":\"Microsoft server-side login is disabled\"}") — hardcoded JSON string.
SecurityConfig's authenticationEntryPoint also uses: response.getWriter().write("{\"error\":\"Unauthorized\"}") — same pattern.

issues.summary: mergeProviderConfig name suggests merging two configs but the function applies defaults to a partial/null input
issues.suggestion: Rename to normalizeProviderConfig or withDefaults to accurately describe applying defaults to a partial server response.
evidence: Function signature: mergeProviderConfig(config?: Partial<ProviderConfig> | null): ProviderConfig
Function body spreads DEFAULT_PROVIDER_CONFIG against the input — it normalizes defaults, not merges two peer configs.
Callers: useProviderConfig.ts uses it as mergeProviderConfig(response) — one argument, not two peers.

issues.summary: Minor: one-implementation interfaces justified by @Profile test pattern
issues.suggestion: No change needed; the pattern is intentional for testability.
evidence: AuthCodeStore interface has one impl (RedisAuthCodeStore) — intentional for @Profile('!test') swapping

issues.summary: google-api-client is a heavy transitive dependency used only for GoogleIdTokenVerifier
issues.suggestion: Replace google-api-client with NimbusJwtDecoder.withJwkSetUri("https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/certs") and validate the audience claim manually, removing the heavy google-api-client transitive dependency.
evidence: pom.xml includes com.google.api-client:google-api-client:2.7.2
Only usage: new GoogleIdTokenVerifier.Builder(new NetHttpTransport(), GsonFactory.getDefaultInstance()).setAudience(...).build()
Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server (already a dependency) has JwtDecoder which could verify Google ID tokens using Google's JWKS endpoint.

issues.summary: UserController.getCurrentUser() returns a 10-entry inline Map instead of a typed response record
issues.suggestion: Create a UserResponse record in controller/dto/ with all fields, and a static UserResponse.from(User user) factory. Return ResponseEntity<UserResponse> instead.
evidence: return userService.findCurrentUser(identity).map(user -> ResponseEntity.ok(Map.of("id", user.getId(), "email", ... 10 entries total)))
The Map keys are untyped strings; a typo in a key name produces runtime failure, not compile-time error.
The null-coalescing for each field (user.getName() != null ? user.getName() : "") repeated 6 times adds noise.

issues.summary: AuthController.cookiePath() null-checks servletContextPath which is already guaranteed non-null by constructor normalization
issues.suggestion: Remove the null check from cookiePath() since the constructor guarantees a non-null value. Simplify to: if (servletContextPath.isBlank() || "/".equals(servletContextPath))
evidence: Constructor: this.servletContextPath = servletContextPath == null ? "" : servletContextPath;
cookiePath(): if (servletContextPath == null || servletContextPath.isBlank() || ...) — the null check is unreachable.

issues.summary: Microsoft challenge session cookie management is spread across two controller endpoints with inline logic
issues.suggestion: Extract ChallengeSessionResolver with methods resolveOrCreate(request, response): String and resolve(request): Optional<String>. Inject into AuthController. This cleans up the seam between the two Microsoft endpoints.
evidence: issueMicrosoftChallenge() calls currentChallengeSessionId(request).orElseGet(() -> createChallengeSessionCookie(response)) inline.
verifyMicrosoftToken() calls currentChallengeSessionId(httpRequest).orElse(null) with its own null check.
The sessionId extraction and cookie creation logic (~30 LOC) lives in AuthController rather than in a dedicated ChallengeSessionResolver component.

issues.summary: ProviderIdentityNormalizer has no unit tests despite containing complex email normalization and guest identity rejection logic
issues.suggestion: Create ProviderIdentityNormalizerTest with cases: valid Google identity, missing sub or email throws, valid Microsoft identity with each of the 3 claim paths, guest #ext# email rejected, email with spaces rejected, email with 2 @ signs rejected.
evidence: normalizeEmail() performs: trim, lowercase, count '@' occurrences, check for spaces, guest '#ext#' rejection — 5 distinct validation rules.
normalizeMicrosoftClaims() uses firstNonBlank across 3 email-like claim keys (email, preferred_username, upn).
No test file for ProviderIdentityNormalizer found in the test tree.
A bug here would allow invalid emails or permit guest Microsoft identities — security-relevant.

issues.summary: MicrosoftTokenVerifier has no unit tests for nonce validation, issuer validation, and audience validation
issues.suggestion: Create MicrosoftTokenVerifierTest with mocked JwtDecoder. Test: correct nonce passes, wrong nonce throws, mismatched audience throws, mismatched issuer throws, v1.0 token version throws.
evidence: validateNonce(), validateIssuer(), validateAudience(), validateVersion() are critical security checks with no corresponding tests.
A wrong nonce allows replay attacks. A wrong issuer allows cross-tenant impersonation.
No MicrosoftTokenVerifierTest file found.

issues.summary: Controllers return Map<String,Object> and ResponseEntity<?> instead of typed response DTOs
issues.suggestion: Create response DTOs: TokenResponse(String token), UserResponse record with all user fields, ProviderConfigResponse with typed Google/Microsoft sub-records. Change return types to ResponseEntity<TokenResponse> etc.
evidence: AuthController methods: public ResponseEntity<?> verifyGoogleToken(...), public ResponseEntity<?> verifyMicrosoftToken(...), public ResponseEntity<?> exchangeCode(...)
All success bodies: Map.of("token", jwt) — repeated inline literal key.
UserController: ResponseEntity<Map<String,Object>> with 10 inline Map.of() entries.
ProviderConfigController: ResponseEntity<Map<String,Object>> with nested LinkedHashMap construction.

issues.summary: Microsoft client-side enabled check duplicated in both /microsoft/challenge and /microsoft/verify endpoints
issues.suggestion: Extract a private assertMicrosoftClientSideEnabled() method that throws a custom FeatureDisabledException, or apply an @PreAuthorize SpEL condition via a custom security expression. Alternatively, handle this at the filter level in MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter for /auth/microsoft/** paths.
evidence: issueMicrosoftChallenge(): if (!rolloutProperties.getMicrosoft().isClientSideEnabled()) return 503
verifyMicrosoftToken(): if (!rolloutProperties.getMicrosoft().isClientSideEnabled()) return 503
Same guard, same response body: Map.of("error", "Microsoft client-side login is disabled")

issues.summary: AuthCodeStore method Javadocs partially restate method names without adding unique insight
issues.suggestion: Revise Javadoc to focus on WHY and edge cases: explain TTL, the security reason for single-use, and that null return requires the caller to handle expired codes.
evidence: '/** Store a JWT and return a single-use authorization code. */' — 'single-use' adds value but 'Store a JWT' restates storeJwt().
'/** Exchange a code for the stored JWT. Returns null if invalid/expired. */' — the null-return contract adds value.

issues.summary: User entity has both loginMethod (String) and lastLoginFlow (AuthFlow enum) representing the same login concept
issues.suggestion: Once downstream consumers read lastLoginFlow, drop loginMethod from the entity and the database schema via a Flyway migration. Add a DB migration to backfill last_login_flow from login_method for existing rows.
evidence: User.loginMethod: @Column(name = "login_method", length = 20) private String loginMethod;
User.lastLoginFlow: @Enumerated(EnumType.STRING) @Column(name = "last_login_flow", length = 20) private AuthFlow lastLoginFlow;
UserService.applyProviderIdentityFields() sets both: user.setLastLoginFlow(identity.loginFlow()); user.setLoginMethod(identity.loginFlow().name());
The two fields store the same value in different types — the String field is the legacy representation.

issues.summary: service package contains 12 files across interfaces, implementations, value records, and domain services without subpackaging
issues.suggestion: Create service/store/ for AuthCodeStore + MicrosoftChallengeStore interfaces and their Redis implementations. Create service/identity/ for NormalizedIdentity, AuthenticatedUserIdentity, and ProviderIdentityNormalizer.
evidence: service/ has: 2 store interfaces, 2 Redis store implementations, 2 identity value records, 1 identity normalizer, 2 token verifiers, 1 JWT service, 1 success handler, 1 user service.
NormalizedIdentity and AuthenticatedUserIdentity are pure domain records that don't depend on service infrastructure.
Redis implementations have @Profile('!test') annotations — a clear deployment-coupling concern distinct from pure service interfaces.

issues.summary: GoogleTokenVerifier constructs a GoogleIdTokenVerifier (with HTTP transport) in the constructor
issues.suggestion: Consider extracting verifier construction to a @Bean factory method in a config class, making the HTTP transport mockable for tests. Alternatively, accept the current pattern as reasonable since no network I/O occurs at startup.
evidence: Constructor: this.verifier = new GoogleIdTokenVerifier.Builder(new NetHttpTransport(), GsonFactory.getDefaultInstance()).setAudience(...).build()
NetHttpTransport is created inline at construction time, creating a dependency on network infrastructure at bean initialization.
No actual network call is made at construction, but the HTTP client is initialized.

issues.summary: UserService.findOrCreateUser() combines lookup, legacy fallback, update, create, and race-condition handling in one method
issues.suggestion: Extract private methods: updateExistingUser(User user, NormalizedIdentity identity) and createNewUser(NormalizedIdentity identity). Keep findOrCreateUser as orchestrator calling these private methods.
evidence: Method does: (1) findByProviderAndProviderUserId, (2) Google legacy fallback findByGoogleId, (3) update existing user fields, (4) create new user, (5) catch DataIntegrityViolationException for concurrent creation.
The update-existing block: 5 setters + lastLoginAt — could be extracted to updateUserFromIdentity(user, identity).
The create-new block: 6 setters + Redis save + race-condition catch — could be extracted to createUserFromIdentity(identity).

issues.summary: MicrosoftChallengeStore interface methods have no Javadoc explaining their contracts
issues.suggestion: Add Javadoc to issueChallenge() explaining the sessionId scoping, TTL of 5 minutes, and the returned MicrosoftChallenge structure. Add Javadoc to consumeNonce() explaining single-use semantics (returning null if not found or already consumed).
evidence: issueChallenge(String sessionId) — no documentation explaining what sessionId is, what the challenge lifecycle is.
consumeNonce(String sessionId, String challengeId) — no documentation explaining single-use semantics or what null return means.

issues.summary: normalizeEmail() has a 5-clause OR condition that is hard to parse at a glance
issues.suggestion: Extract validation conditions into named boolean variables or private predicate methods: boolean hasExactlyOneAt = ...; boolean hasNoSpaces = ...; boolean hasNonEmptyParts = atIndex > 0 && atIndex < normalized.length() - 1;. Then: if (normalized.isBlank() || !hasExactlyOneAt || !hasNoSpaces || !hasNonEmptyParts)
evidence: if (normalized.isBlank() || normalized.chars().filter(ch -> ch == '@').count() != 1 || normalized.contains(" ") || atIndex <= 0 || atIndex == normalized.length() - 1)

issues.summary: Frontend useAuth UserProfile interface is missing fields that the backend /user/me endpoint now returns
issues.suggestion: Update UserProfile interface in useAuth.ts to include provider: string, providerUserId: string, lastLoginFlow: string fields. Move UserProfile to types/auth.ts for co-location with other auth types.
evidence: UserProfile interface: { id, email, name, pictureUrl, loginMethod, createdAt, lastLoginAt }
Backend UserController returns: id, email, name, pictureUrl, loginMethod, provider, providerUserId, lastLoginFlow, createdAt, lastLoginAt
Missing from UserProfile: provider, providerUserId, lastLoginFlow — these are now available but untypeable in the frontend.

add.header: Clean Spring layered architecture with no cycles
add.description: The codebase respects controller → service → repository → model direction throughout. No circular imports. Config is legitimately cross-cutting.

add.header: service package is an umbrella without subpackaging
add.description: The service package co-locates interfaces, Redis implementations, identity records, and verifier services. As the service layer grows this will impede navigation.

add.header: Frontend has clean page/component/hook decomposition
add.description: The React frontend correctly separates pages, components, hooks, api client, and types — good structural discipline.

add.header: AuthController is an overgrown orchestration hub
add.description: With 5 endpoints and 8 injected dependencies, AuthController violates single-responsibility; it owns Google, Microsoft, exchange, and logout in one file.

add.header: Consistent constructor injection across all Spring components
add.description: Every Spring bean uses constructor injection rather than field injection — a consistent and testability-friendly convention.

add.header: Service layer consistently uses IllegalArgumentException for validation failures
add.description: Token verifiers and identity normalizers use a consistent throw-IllegalArgumentException pattern for invalid input, making controller error handling predictable.

add.header: AuthCodeStore breaks throw convention with null-return
add.description: The null-return contract forces a different handling pattern in AuthController compared to the try-catch used for all other service failures.

add.header: Domain vocabulary is precise and consistent
add.description: Contract, identity, provider, flow, mint, rollout — these domain terms are used correctly and consistently across all layers without abbreviation drift.

add.header: Single-implementation interfaces are justified by @Profile test pattern
add.description: AuthCodeStore and MicrosoftChallengeStore exist as interfaces for @Profile('!test') swapping — a legitimate testability abstraction.

add.header: No duplicate-purpose dependencies in backend or frontend
add.description: Each domain (JWT, OAuth, HTTP, DB) uses exactly one library across the entire stack. No conflicting versions or alternative implementations for the same concern.

add.header: MicrosoftTokenVerifier has exemplary method decomposition
add.description: Validation logic is split into four focused private methods (validateAudience, validateIssuer, validateVersion, validateNonce), each with a single concern and clear throw on failure.

add.header: OAuth2 success handler has clean data-translation seam
add.description: The flow from OAuth2User attributes → ProviderIdentityNormalizer → NormalizedIdentity → UserService is a clean one-directional data transformation with a single translation point.

add.header: JWT service and filter tests provide solid core coverage
add.description: JwtTokenServiceTest, JwtAuthenticationFilterTest, and UserServiceTest cover the core authentication pipeline with behavioral tests.

add.header: Critical gaps in identity normalization and Microsoft token validation
add.description: ProviderIdentityNormalizer and MicrosoftTokenVerifier — the two classes with security-relevant validation logic — have no unit tests.

add.header: Consistent token response shape across all auth flows
add.description: All successful auth flows return {"token": "..."}  consistently — Google client-side, Microsoft client-side, and code exchange all use the same structure.

add.header: Startup IdentityContractGuard prevents authorization misconfiguration
add.description: IdentityContractGuard validates at boot time that Microsoft SSO requires V2_ONLY identity contract mode, preventing runtime authorization failures from configuration errors.

add.header: Comments explain non-obvious design decisions consistently
add.description: Key comments explain WHY (GETDEL atomicity, cookie truncation for security logging). No restating comments on obvious operations.

add.header: V1→V2 JWT migration is actively managed with rollout flags
add.description: AuthRolloutProperties and IdentityContractGuard provide controlled rollout of the V2 JWT contract. The migration is in progress but well-instrumented.

add.header: loginMethod String and lastLoginFlow AuthFlow duplicate the same concept
add.description: Both fields store login flow info (SERVER_SIDE/CLIENT_SIDE). The String field is a migration artifact that should be dropped once lastLoginFlow is confirmed as the canonical source.

add.header: Frontend package structure is appropriate for project size
add.description: Frontend uses api/, components/, hooks/, pages/, types/ — correct granularity for a small project with ~12 source files.

add.header: Constructor injection prevents initialization ordering issues
add.description: All Spring beans use constructor injection — Spring guarantees dependency resolution order, eliminating hidden initialization coupling.

add.header: MicrosoftTokenVerifier has exemplary single-responsibility design
add.description: Four focused private validation methods plus a factory for the decoder produces a class with clear, testable responsibilities.

add.header: AuthCodeStore has well-documented interface contracts
add.description: Both storeJwt and exchangeCode have Javadoc specifying single-use semantics, null return semantics, and the purpose of the operation.

add.header: JwtTokenService version-dispatching logic is clear and intention-revealing
add.description: The parseAuthenticatedUser method uses clear early-return guards for each version path with explicit null-check and version-mismatch handling before the happy path.

add.header: Domain records and enums provide strong type discipline
add.description: AuthProvider, AuthFlow, JwtMintMode, IdentityContractMode enums prevent magic string comparisons. NormalizedIdentity and AuthenticatedUserIdentity records enforce field completeness at construction.

add.header: Frontend UserProfile interface is stale relative to backend API
add.description: useAuth.ts UserProfile is missing provider, providerUserId, and lastLoginFlow that /user/me now returns, causing TypeScript to lose type coverage for these new fields.
